title: Family: Talking About the People Closest to You
objectives: Name close family members in Spanish.
Use mi, mis, tu, and tus with family words.
Talk about spouse, children, parents, siblings, and in-laws.
Use tengo, tiene, and se llama in simple family sentences.
Describe your family politely in a real conversation.

vocabulary.tip: A core word for personal conversations.
Use esposa for wife. Mi means my.
Use esposo for husband. In everyday speech, marido is also common in some regions.
Can mean sons or children in a mixed group.
Use el hijo for the son.
Use la hija for the daughter.
Can mean parents or fathers depending on context.
Madre is neutral or formal. Mama is warmer and informal.
Padre is neutral or formal. Papa is warmer and informal.
Can mean brothers or siblings in a mixed group.
Use hermano for brother.
Use hermana for sister.
Can mean grandfathers or grandparents in a mixed group.
Abuelito is a warmer affectionate version.
Abuelita is a warmer affectionate version.
vocabulary.exampleSpanish: Mi abuela vive con mi tia.
vocabulary.exampleEnglish: My grandmother lives with my aunt.

vocabulary.tip: Usually means parents-in-law.
Use mi suegro for my father-in-law.
Use mi suegra for my mother-in-law.
Use cunado for brother-in-law.
Use cunada for sister-in-law.
Tia is also used warmly for close family friends in some families.
In Spain, tio can also mean guy in casual slang, but here it means uncle.
Use prima for a female cousin.
Use primo for a male cousin.
Use sobrino for nephew.
vocabulary.exampleEnglish: My nephew is five years old.

vocabulary.tip: Use sobrina for niece.
Use mi before one family member.
Use mis before plural family words.
Use tu before one family member.
Use tus before plural family words.

familyPhraseVariants.whenUsed: Use esposa anywhere. Mujer can mean wife in Spain and some regions, but esposa is clearer for learners.
Use esposo anywhere. Marido is common for husband in Spain and much of Latin America.
Use hijos for children when at least one child is male or when gender is not specified.
Padres is neutral. Papas is warmer and common in Mexico for parents.

familyPhraseVariants.naturalEverydayVersion: Mi suegra es muy buena onda.
familyPhraseVariants.mexicanSpanishVersion: Mi suegra es muy buena onda.
familyPhraseVariants.whenUsed: Amable is safe and universal. Buena onda is Mexican and casual. Maja is Spain Spanish and casual.
Use tu before one family member. Use tus for plural family members.
Hermanos can mean siblings when talking generally.
Both versions are natural. Tengo una familia grande focuses on having a large family.
Use casada for a woman and casado for a man.
This is a warm, natural family sentence. Amar exists, but querer is very common for family affection.

grammar.explanation: Use mi and tu with one family member. Use mis and tus with plural family words. Use tengo for I have, tiene for he/she has, and se llama for is named.

notes.title: Mi / mis
notes.text: Use mi before one thing: mi esposa. Use mis before more than one: mis padres.

notes.title: Tu / tus
notes.text: Use tu before one thing: tu hermano. Use tus before more than one: tus hijos.

notes.title: Tengo / tiene
notes.text: Tengo means I have. Tiene means he has, she has, or formal you have.

notes.title: Se llama
notes.text: Use se llama to say someone is named: Mi hija se llama Sofia.

notes.title: Plural family words
notes.text: Many family plurals use the masculine form for mixed groups: hijos can mean children, padres can mean parents, hermanos can mean siblings.

examples.spanish: Tengo dos hijos y mis padres viven cerca.
examples.english: I have two children and my parents live nearby.

grammar.practicePrompt: Tell Carlos about three people in your family using mi, mis, tengo, and se llama.

lines.speaker: Learner
lines.spanish: Tengo un hermano y una hermana.
lines.english: I have one brother and one sister.

lines.speaker: Carlos
lines.english: How nice. What are their names?

lines.speaker: Carlos
lines.english: Do you have aunts and uncles?

lines.speaker: Learner
lines.spanish: Si, tengo una tia y un tio.
lines.english: Yes, I have an aunt and an uncle.

lines.speaker: Learner
lines.spanish: Mi familia es pequena. Tengo una esposa y dos hijos.
lines.english: My family is small. I have a wife and two children.

lines.speaker: Carlos
lines.spanish: Muy bien. ¿Como se llaman tus hijos?
lines.english: Very good. What are your children's names?

pronunciation.focus: Family word rhythm, ñ, and plural endings.
tips: The ñ in cunado/cunada would normally be written with ñ in Spanish and sounds like ny.
Plural -s is light but present: padres, hijos, hermanos.
In familia, keep all vowels clear: fa-mi-lia.
Suegro and suegra start with a smooth swe sound.
Abuelo has four vowel sounds: a-bue-lo.

items.note: The h is silent in hijos.
Pronounce the final s in mis.
The h is silent in hermano.
The h is silent in hermana.
Practice the swe sound in suegros.
Keep sue-gra as two clear parts.

culture.title: Family talk is warm, personal, and polite
culture.text: In many Spanish-speaking families, asking about family can be a warm way to connect. Use polite and positive words when talking about in-laws, especially early in a relationship. Terms like mama, papa, abuelita, and abuelito can feel affectionate, while madre, padre, abuela, and abuelo are more neutral. When meeting family members for the first time, a greeting plus mucho gusto goes a long way.

nativeSpeech.title: How native speakers talk about family
notes: Hijos often means children, not only sons.
Hermanos can mean siblings when the group is mixed or unspecified.
In Spain, mi mujer and mi marido are common for my wife and my husband.
In Mexico, buena onda is a casual way to say someone is nice or cool.

commonMistakes.mistake: Using mi with plural family words.
commonMistakes.correction: Use mis padres, mis hijos, mis hermanos.
commonMistakes.explanation: Mi is singular. Mis is plural.

commonMistakes.mistake: Using tu with plural family words.
commonMistakes.correction: Use tus padres or tus hijos.
commonMistakes.explanation: Tu is singular. Tus is plural.

commonMistakes.correction: Hijos can mean children when talking about a mixed group or children in general.
commonMistakes.explanation: Spanish often uses masculine plural forms for mixed groups.

commonMistakes.mistake: Saying mi esposa se nombre Ana.
commonMistakes.correction: Say mi esposa se llama Ana.
commonMistakes.explanation: Use se llama to say someone is named.

commonMistakes.correction: Use tengo for I have and tiene for he/she has.
commonMistakes.explanation: The verb changes with the person.

quiz.prompt: How do you say my parents?
How do you say my daughter?
Translate: Mi esposa se llama Ana.

speakingChallenge.prompt: Tell Carlos about your spouse using mi and se llama.
Tell Carlos whether you have children.
Talk about your parents using mis.
Give Carlos a short family introduction with at least five family words.
speakingChallenge.exampleAnswer: Tengo una esposa, dos hijos, una hermana, un hermano y mis padres viven cerca.

realLifeMission.title: Real Life Mission
realLifeMission.mission: Tell Carlos about your family using at least five family words and two possessive words.
successCriteria: Use at least five family words, such as esposa, hijos, padres, hermano, or suegros.
Use at least two possessive words: mi, mis, tu, or tus.
Use tengo or tiene at least once.
Use se llama for at least one family member if possible.
